meta.name: CES-D Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale
meta.fullName: Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale
meta.description: The CES-D is a 20-item self-report depression scale developed for use in the general population. It measures the frequency of depressive symptoms experienced during the past week and is one of the most widely used instruments in epidemiological research on depression.
meta.author: Radloff (1977)
meta.reference: Radloff LS. The CES-D Scale: A self-report depression scale for research in the general population. Appl Psychol Meas. 1977;1(3):385-401.
meta.instruction: Below is a list of ways you might have felt or behaved. Please indicate how often you have felt this way during the past week.

commonChoices.label: Rarely or none of the time (less than 1 day)
Some or a little of the time (1-2 days)
Occasionally or a moderate amount of time (3-4 days)
Most or all of the time (5-7 days)

questions.text: I was bothered by things that usually don't bother me
I did not feel like eating; my appetite was poor
I felt that I could not shake off the blues even with help from my family or friends
I felt that I was just as good as other people
I had trouble keeping my mind on what I was doing
I felt that everything I did was an effort
I felt hopeful about the future
I thought my life had been a failure
I felt that people disliked me

ranges.label: Normal
ranges.description: Your score is within the normal range. No significant depressive symptoms detected. This indicates that your mood has been relatively stable recently, with no significant signs of persistent sadness or loss of interest.
suggestions: Continue maintaining a healthy lifestyle
Keep up regular social activities and exercise
Occasional mood fluctuations are a normal part of life
Maintain regular social activities and hobbies
Set aside time each day for activities you enjoy

ranges.label: Mild Depression
ranges.description: Your score suggests mild depressive symptoms. These may be affecting your daily life to some extent. These mild symptoms may occasionally affect your energy and motivation, but they typically do not interfere significantly with daily functioning.
suggestions: Pay attention to your emotional well-being
Maintain regular sleep, exercise, and social routines
Try talking to a trusted friend or family member
If symptoms persist for more than two weeks, consider consulting a professional
Try 30 minutes of aerobic exercise daily, such as walking or cycling

ranges.label: Moderate Depression
ranges.description: Your score indicates moderate depressive symptoms that may be significantly affecting your daily functioning. At this level, feelings of low mood may cause persistent fatigue and reduced interest in activities, potentially affecting daily life and relationships.
suggestions: Consider consulting a mental health professional
Maintain a structured daily routine
Engage in regular physical activity
Avoid isolation; stay connected with supportive people
Consider psychotherapy (e.g., cognitive behavioral therapy)

ranges.label: Severe Depression
ranges.description: Your score suggests severe depressive symptoms. Professional help is strongly recommended. Your score suggests notable emotional distress that may be significantly affecting your quality of life. Reaching out to a mental health professional is recommended.
suggestions: Seek consultation with a mental health professional as soon as possible
If you have thoughts of self-harm, please call a crisis hotline immediately (e.g., 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline)
Do not stay alone; inform a trusted person about your situation
Professional treatment, including therapy and/or medication, can be very effective
Schedule an appointment with a mental health professional as soon as possible
